title: Origami masu box
subtitle: Folding a traditional Japanese square box from a single sheet
excerpt: The masu box is a traditional Japanese origami form: a square open box folded from a single sheet, named after the traditional wooden measure used in Japan. Two boxes, one folded from a sheet 1 cm smaller than the other, nest as a lid and base. The fold sequence is precise, with eight equal divisions as the starting grid, and every fold meeting a marked point.
sourceNotes: The masu box fold is a traditional Japanese origami model. The design is documented in origami collections from the mid-twentieth century and draws on the centuries-old tradition of Japanese paper folding (origami and kirigami). The masu box appears in Akira Yoshizawa's origami documentation and in multiple pre-1970 origami manuals as a standard introductory project.

glossaryTerms.definition: A traditional Japanese square wooden box used as a measure for rice and sake. The origami masu box replicates its square form in folded paper. Masu are still used ceremonially in Japan.
A fold in which the paper is folded toward you (down into a valley shape). In origami notation, represented by a dashed line. The most common fold type.
A fold in which the paper is folded away from you (up into a peak shape). In origami notation, represented by a dot-dash line.

content.text: box is one of the most satisfying origami projects because it has a clear practical use. Fold two from sheets differing by 1 cm (a 20 cm and a 19 cm sheet) and the smaller box becomes the lid. Use good kami or slightly heavier paper for a box that holds its shape; thin paper produces a floppy result.

items.name: Kami origami paper, 20 cm (for box) and 19 cm or 20 cm (for lid)
items.substitutions: Duo paper to show both colours when assembled as lid and box; heavyweight cartridge cut to a square

content.text: Start with the coloured side up.
the sheet in half both ways and unfold, this gives the centre point and the horizontal and vertical centre lines. Fold each edge to the centre line and unfold. You now have a 4×4 grid on the sheet.
Fold all four corners in to the centre point (a blintz base fold). Crease firmly.
Fold the top and bottom edges to the centre line and unfold. Fold the left and right edges to the centre line and leave them folded.
Open out the left and right triangular flaps (the folded corners). Open the top and bottom edges outward. The crease at each corner becomes a
Lift the two open sides upward to form the box walls. The triangular corner flaps fold inward and flatten to form the two remaining walls. Press all four walls firm and crease all base corners.
Fold the short flap panels over the top of the end walls to lock the structure. Crease firmly with the bone folder.

Repeat the exact sequence with a sheet 5 mm to 10 mm larger (or smaller, depending on which is the lid). A 19 cm sheet produces a lid for a 20 cm box; a 20 cm sheet produces a loose-fitting lid for a 19 cm box. The fit depends on paper thickness, test with a scrap sheet.

items.symptom: The box walls are floppy and will not stand straight
items.cause: Paper is too thin, or the corner creases were not sharp enough.
items.fix: Use kami 20 cm (90 gsm equivalent) rather than thin printer paper. Crease all folds firmly with a bone folder before opening the box.

items.symptom: The lid does not fit, too tight or too loose
items.cause: Both box and lid folded from the same sheet size.
items.fix: The lid sheet should be 5 to 10 mm larger than the box sheet for a snug fit. Adjust the size difference based on the paper thickness: thicker paper needs a slightly larger size difference.
